key_points: Image formation by spherical mirrors depends on the position of the object with respect to the focus and centre of curvature
Concave mirrors can produce real and inverted images or virtual and erect images depending on object distance
Convex mirrors always form virtual, erect and diminished images
Refraction follows Snell’s law which relates angles of incidence and refraction to refractive index
Apparent depth occurs when light bends at the boundary of two media causing objects under water to appear raised
examples: Image formed by a concave mirror when the object is placed between focus and pole is virtual and magnified
Dentists use concave mirrors to obtain enlarged images of teeth
The bottom of a swimming pool appearing shallower due to refraction
Mirage formation in deserts caused by bending of light in layers of air
formulas: Magnification relation → m = hᵢ / hₒ = -v / u
Refractive index → n = sin i / sin r = c / v
Relation between focal length and radius of curvature → f = R/2
exam_tips: Practice solving numerical problems involving mirror formula and magnification
Learn to apply correct sign convention while solving problems
Understand how refractive index affects bending of light
Draw accurate ray diagrams for different positions of objects
